summary:
The founding of St. Petersburg (May 27, 1703 AD) by [[event:2349|Peter the Great]] was one of the most audacious acts of imperial will in history: the creation of a new capital city from scratch on the marshy, flooded delta of the Neva River in territory recently seized from Sweden during the [[event:2348|Great Northern War]] — a site with no existing infrastructure, in a climate of extreme cold and floods, built primarily through forced serf labor. Peter designated the site — strategically positioned to give Russia direct sea access to the Baltic and thus to western Europe — as Russia's new capital and 'window to Europe,' intending it to symbolize and drive Russia's transformation from an inward-looking Muscovite state to an outward-facing European empire.

The construction cost was staggering in human terms: contemporary estimates suggest 100,000 or more conscripted laborers died building the city's foundations, canals, and first buildings on the swampy, frequently flooded ground — earning the city the grim epithet 'built on bones.' Peter had European architects (particularly Italian and Dutch) design the city on a grid plan with wide boulevards, canals, and monumental buildings in Baroque European style — physically embodying his vision of a Westernized Russia. The Peter and Paul Fortress (1703), the first structure, became the burial site of Russian emperors; the Winter Palace, Admiralty, and later the Hermitage created one of Europe's great imperial urban environments. St. Petersburg served as Russia's capital from 1712 until 1918, when the Bolshevik government moved back to Moscow. Renamed Petrograd in 1914, then Leningrad in 1924, and restored to St. Petersburg in 1991, the city remains one of Europe's most beautiful — a testament to Peter's transformative ambition.
